explicit_text: I met Muriel MacGregor in the fall of 1961 when I moved to Estes Park. She was a soft-spoken woman who kept cattle on her property near Black Canyon. Despite many stories about her, like carrying dead calves in her car, I found her to be kind and lonely, reaching out for friendship. Over the years, especially after her stroke around 1949, I helped her with daily tasks and took her to the chiropractor in Loveland. She loved visiting with my grandchildren and cared deeply for her cattle, often driving around the ranch to check on them.

explicit_text: I moved to Estes Park in October 1961 and lived near Black Canyon Ranch. Estes Park was where I met Muriel MacGregor and where much of my story unfolded. The town had places like Riverside Market near Fun City and was close to areas like Mary's Lake and High Drive where I eventually bought a house. Estes Park was a community with ranches, restaurants, and a close-knit feel, especially during the 1960s when my husband was building condos nearby.

explicit_text: When I first arrived in Estes Park in 1961, we lived at Black Canyon Ranch, which was actually a dude ranch with horses and cabins. My husband was building condos there during that winter. The ranch was near the Black Canyon Restaurant and was surrounded by the MacGregor property. It was a beautiful place overlooking Black Canyon and a hub for local ranching and community life.

explicit_text: Dr. Curry Myers owned property near the Black Canyon Ranch where we lived in 1961. His property was surrounded by the MacGregor land, and the area was known as Black Canyon Hills. Though I didn't interact much with Dr. Myers personally, his land was a reference point in the community and close to where my husband was building condos.

explicit_text: Evergreen Lane was a street near the MacGregor Ranch and just across from the entrance to the Black Canyon Restaurant. In the early 1960s, I saw an ad for a house to rent there and contacted Muriel MacGregor about it. Although the house was too small for us, it was clear Muriel wanted us to rent it badly, showing her loneliness and desire for friendship. Evergreen Lane was a convenient location close to my husband's work and the ranch.

explicit_text: After deciding the house on Evergreen Lane was too small, we bought a home on High Drive in Estes Park. This was around the winter of 1961-1962, and it gave us more room for our family, including my mother-in-law. High Drive was a comfortable place to live while I continued visiting Muriel MacGregor and managing life around the ranches.

explicit_text: Mary's Lake was near where Muriel MacGregor had a cabin, and it was the place where she had a fall on the ice that led to her stroke around 1949. She told me about this incident, which affected her mobility for the rest of her life. The lake area was part of the broader Estes Park region where many ranches and cabins were located.

explicit_text: Riverside Market, formerly known as Richardson’s, was the grocery store near Fun City in Estes Park that delivered groceries to Muriel MacGregor. They provided delivery service for at least three years until her death, which was a great help since she couldn’t shop for herself. The delivery men, including Barney and J. Bruns, were familiar faces and friends of my family.

explicit_text: Virginia Denning knew Muriel MacGregor and her family well. Her parents rented the Quiner House on the ranch for about ten summers, and Virginia often talked about the Boyce children, Muriel’s maiden family. Though Virginia was married and no longer living on the ranch when I arrived, she was part of the local history connected to Muriel.
